short: A verb that governs another verb in the infinitive: dovere (must), potere (can), volere (want).
long: Italian modals dovere, potere, volere take an infinitive complement: 'Devo studiare', 'Posso uscire?', 'Voglio mangiare'. In compound tenses, the modal can take its own default auxiliary (avere) or inherit the auxiliary of the governed infinitive — see modal auxiliary inheritance.

short: A modal verb (dovere, potere, volere) taking the auxiliary of the infinitive it governs instead of its own default avere.
long: When a modal precedes an infinitive that selects essere (andare, partire, arrivare, reflexives), prescriptive Italian has the modal also take essere: 'sono dovuto andare', 'è voluta partire' (with participle agreement). Colloquial Italian increasingly uses avere with the modal regardless: 'ho dovuto andare'. Both are now widely accepted; the prescriptive form is preferred in writing.

short: When a verb's contracted infinitive 'opens out' into a longer stem in the past participle.
long: Several Italian verbs are descended from longer Latin infinitives that contracted over time: bere from bevere, dire from dicere, fare from facere, porre from ponere, tradurre from traducere. Their past participles use the older, expanded stem: bevuto, detto, fatto, posto, tradotto. The contracted-infinitive class is small but high-frequency.

short: A verb that takes avere when used transitively and essere when used intransitively.
long: Some Italian verbs change auxiliary with use: correre, passare, cambiare, saltare, volare. 'Ho corso la maratona' (transitive, ran the race → avere); 'sono corso a casa' (intransitive, motion → essere). The choice reflects whether the verb is acting on a direct object or describing motion or change.

short: An intransitive verb describing directed movement: andare, venire, partire, arrivare, uscire, entrare, salire, scendere, tornare.
long: Italian verbs of motion take essere as their auxiliary in compound tenses, and the past participle agrees with the subject. The class is fairly closed. Often abbreviated as 'motion intransitive' in technical descriptions.

short: A verb describing a transition: nascere (be born), morire (die), diventare (become), restare (remain).
long: Italian verbs of change of state take essere as their auxiliary in compound tenses, like verbs of motion. The past participle agrees with the subject. Examples: nascere (sono nato), morire (è morto), diventare (sono diventato), restare (sono restato).

short: Getting the auxiliary form right for the subject's person and number, distinct from getting the auxiliary family right.
long: In a compound tense, the auxiliary must match the subject's person and number: 'Marco ha parlato' (3sg), not 'Marco hanno parlato' (3pl). A learner can pick the right auxiliary family (avere) but produce the wrong form. The Linguics taxonomy distinguishes auxiliary family choice from auxiliary person agreement because the errors have different remedies.

short: In compound tenses, certain adverbs (già, ancora, mai, sempre, appena) sit between the auxiliary and the past participle.
long: Italian places a small set of high-frequency time/frequency adverbs between the auxiliary and the past participle: 'ho già mangiato', 'non sono mai stato a Roma', 'sono appena arrivata'. Anglophone learners often produce 'ho mangiato già' or 'già ho mangiato', both wrong. The rule applies to già, ancora, mai, sempre, appena, anche, and a few others.

short: The unmarked auxiliary choice in the passato prossimo. Used for transitive verbs and most intransitives that don't fit the motion/change/reflexive classes.
long: Most Italian verbs take avere as their auxiliary in compound tenses: 'ho mangiato', 'hanno lavorato', 'abbiamo parlato'. Essere is used only for the marked classes (verbs of motion, change of state, reflexive verbs, and a few others like piacere, bastare, mancare). When in doubt, avere is the default.

short: The rule for choosing English tense when translating an Italian passato prossimo: simple past when a definite past-time adverb is present, present perfect otherwise.
long: Italian passato prossimo covers ground that English splits into simple past and present perfect. With a definite past-time adverb (ieri, l'anno scorso, due giorni fa), English uses simple past: 'I ate yesterday'. With durative or up-to-now adverbs (mai, ancora, già, appena), English uses present perfect: 'I've never eaten sushi'. Present perfect with a definite past-time adverb is ungrammatical in English.

short: An infix used by some -ire verbs in the present tense: capire conjugates capisco/capisci/capisce/capiamo/capite/capiscono.
long: A subset of Italian -ire verbs insert -isc- between the stem and the ending in the three singular forms and the third-person plural of the present tense: capire, finire, preferire, pulire, spedire. The infix does not appear in the noi/voi forms (capiamo, capite) or in the past participle (regular capito). Taught alongside -ire conjugation as a near-default for many common -ire verbs.

short: When the partitive ne precedes the verb in a compound tense, the past participle agrees with the implicit referent.
long: Italian's partitive pronoun ne triggers participle agreement when it precedes the verb: 'Hai mangiato delle mele? Sì, ne ho mangiate tre' (mangiate agrees with mele, f.pl). Without preceding ne, the participle is invariable: 'Ho mangiato tre mele'. The rule parallels preceding-DOP agreement.

short: In compound tenses, 'non' sits immediately before the auxiliary: 'non ho mangiato', not 'ho non mangiato'.
long: Italian places 'non' before the entire verb cluster, including the auxiliary. Anglophone learners often produce 'ho non mangiato' on the model of 'have not eaten' — wrong. Adverbs like ancora, mai, sempre may follow 'non' and the auxiliary: 'non ho ancora finito', 'non sono mai stato a Roma'.

short: Italian's -are verb class. The largest and most regular conjugation: parlare, mangiare, lavorare.
long: The vast majority of Italian verbs end in -are. They follow predictable patterns across all tenses: present parlo/parli/parla/parliamo/parlate/parlano; passato prossimo participle in -ato. New verbs entering Italian (chattare, googlare) join this class by default.

short: Italian's -ere verb class. Many have irregular past participles: prendere→preso, scrivere→scritto, vedere→visto.
long: Italian -ere verbs are mostly the descendants of Latin's second and third conjugations. The regular ones form -uto: venduto, ricevuto, creduto. Common irregulars: prendere→preso, mettere→messo, scrivere→scritto, leggere→letto, vedere→visto, fare→fatto. About a third of -ere verbs are irregular.

short: Italian's -ire verb class. Regular past participle in -ito (dormito, finito, sentito).
long: The smallest of the three Italian conjugations. Regular past participle in -ito. Many -ire verbs use the -isc- pattern in the present tense (capire→capisco). A few have irregular participles: aprire→aperto, venire→venuto, morire→morto.

short: When a subject combines 'tu' with another third-person element (tu e Maria), the verb takes the voi (2pl) form.
long: Italian collapses a mixed second-and-third-person subject to second-plural: 'Tu e Maria avete visto il film' (not hanno). The same pattern applies to 'io e X' collapsing to noi. The diagnostic miss is defaulting to a third-person form because of the third-person component (Maria).
